paradigms.name: Greedy
paradigms.coreIdea: At each step, make the locally optimal choice and never reconsider it. A greedy works when the problem has the greedy choice property -- a local optimum extends to a global optimum -- and optimal substructure.
whenItApplies: Problem asks for a minimum/maximum over a set of choices (scheduling, allocation, covering)
Each decision is irreversible and independent of future decisions
An exchange argument can prove that swapping any two adjacent choices can't improve the result
Interval problems, task scheduling, coin change with canonical denominations

subVariants.name: Sort-then-scan
subVariants.keyMechanism: Sort by one key, greedily consume in order
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Activity selection, meeting rooms

subVariants.name: Heap-maintained frontier
subVariants.keyMechanism: A priority queue tracks the best available choice at each step
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Dijkstra, Prim's MST, K closest points

subVariants.name: Exchange argument / inversion
subVariants.keyMechanism: Prove any deviation from greedy can be "uncrossed" without loss
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Job sequencing by deadline, Huffman coding

subVariants.name: Fractional relaxation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Take as much of the best item as fits, then next-best
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Fractional knapsack

subVariants.name: Two-pass greedy
subVariants.keyMechanism: One left-to-right pass + one right-to-left pass, results combined
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Candy distribution, trapping rain water

subVariants.name: Lazy deletion greedy
subVariants.keyMechanism: Items are "removed" by marking rather than restructuring; heap pops skip stale entries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Task scheduler with cooldown, sliding window maximum

redFlags: The problem has a "0/1" or all-or-nothing choice (greedy usually fails -- DP needed)
Counter-examples exist where the locally best choice leads to a globally worse outcome
The problem involves dependencies between choices (use graph/DP instead)

paradigms.name: Dynamic Programming
paradigms.coreIdea: Break the problem into overlapping subproblems, solve each once, and store results. DP works when the problem has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems -- the hallmark distinction from divide-and-conquer.
whenItApplies: Counting, minimizing, maximizing over an exponential decision space
"How many ways", "minimum cost", "longest/shortest" phrasing
State can be compactly represented and transitions are well-defined
Choices interact (unlike greedy where they're independent)

subVariants.name: Top-down memoization
subVariants.keyMechanism: Recursive with a cache; natural for irregular state spaces
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Word break, climbing stairs

subVariants.name: Bottom-up tabulation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Iterative fill of a table in dependency order; avoids stack overhead
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Longest common subsequence, edit distance

subVariants.name: 1D rolling array
subVariants.keyMechanism: Current row depends only on previous row; reduce O(n^2) space to O(n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: 0-1 knapsack (space-optimized)

subVariants.name: Interval DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: State = (left, right) subarray or subrange; fill diagonally
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Matrix chain multiplication, burst balloons, palindrome partitioning

subVariants.name: Bitmask DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: State includes a bitmask of which items have been used; handles small N with combinatorial choice
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Travelling salesman (small N), minimum XOR cover, assignment problems

subVariants.name: Digit DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: State tracks position + tight constraint + accumulated property; counts integers in a range satisfying a property
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Count numbers with digit sum = K in [L, R]

subVariants.name: Tree DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: DP runs on tree structure; state transitions along parent-child edges
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Max independent set on tree, diameter, rerooting technique

subVariants.name: Profile DP
subVariants.keyMechanism: State encodes the "frontier" shape between processed and unprocessed; common in grid tiling
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Broken profile DP for domino tiling

subVariants.name: DP with monotone queue / divide & conquer optimization
subVariants.keyMechanism: Transition cost satisfies the concave/convex SMAWK condition; reduces O(n^2) to O(n log n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Optimal BST, certain knapsack variants

redFlags: State space is too large to store (use heuristics or approximation instead)
No overlapping subproblems (pure divide-and-conquer is better)
Problem requires online decisions with no future lookahead (use greedy or sliding window)

paradigms.name: Divide and Conquer
paradigms.coreIdea: Split the problem into independent subproblems, solve recursively, then combine. Unlike DP, subproblems do not overlap -- solving them twice would give the same result.
whenItApplies: Problem naturally decomposes into halves or k-parts with no shared state
Combining partial solutions is efficient relative to solving the whole
The problem has a recursive geometric or structural self-similarity
"Find in sorted array", "build a balanced structure", "aggregate over a range"

subVariants.name: Binary search
subVariants.keyMechanism: Eliminate half the search space per step; requires monotone predicate
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Search in rotated array, find peak, capacity to ship packages

subVariants.name: Merge-based
subVariants.keyMechanism: Divide, recurse, merge with cross-partition contribution counted at merge
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Merge sort, count inversions, sort colors

subVariants.name: Randomized partition
subVariants.keyMechanism: Pick random pivot; expected O(n log n) or O(n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Quicksort, quickselect (kth largest)

subVariants.name: Recursive geometric
subVariants.keyMechanism: Partition space recursively; combine nearest-pair or range queries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Closest pair of points, k-d tree construction

subVariants.name: Matrix exponentiation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Express recurrence as matrix multiply; fast-power gives O(log n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Fibonacci in O(log n), linear recurrence of fixed order

subVariants.name: Segment tree build
subVariants.keyMechanism: Build balanced tree over array; each node aggregates a range
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Range min/max/sum queries, lazy propagation

subVariants.name: Parallel binary search
subVariants.keyMechanism: Binary search simultaneously over multiple independent queries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Offline range queries, persistent structures

redFlags: Subproblems are not independent (overlapping -- use DP)
Combining results is as expensive as solving the whole (no asymptotic gain)
Problem requires processing items in a specific order (use sweep line or DP)

paradigms.name: Graph Traversal
paradigms.coreIdea: Model the problem as a graph of states and edges, then traverse it to answer reachability, shortest path, ordering, or connectivity questions.
whenItApplies: Problem involves relationships, dependencies, or transitions between discrete states
"Can we reach X from Y?", "shortest sequence of moves", "ordering with dependencies"
The state space is naturally node-shaped (cells, nodes, configurations)
Implicit graphs where nodes are problem states and edges are valid transitions

subVariants.name: BFS (unweighted shortest path)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Level-by-level expansion; guarantees fewest edges
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Word ladder, 0-1 BFS on grid, multi-source BFS

subVariants.name: Dijkstra (weighted shortest path)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Priority queue, always expand cheapest frontier node
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Network delay time, path with minimum effort

subVariants.name: Bellman-Ford
subVariants.keyMechanism: Relax all edges N-1 times; handles negative weights, detects negative cycles
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Cheapest flights within K stops, currency arbitrage

subVariants.name: DFS (topological / cycle detection)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Post-order DFS; reverse post-order = topological sort
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Course schedule, alien dictionary

subVariants.name: Tarjan's / Kosaraju's SCC
subVariants.keyMechanism: Two-pass DFS or stack-based; finds strongly connected components
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Critical connections, redundant connections

subVariants.name: Floyd-Warshall
subVariants.keyMechanism: All-pairs shortest paths; O(V^3) but simple
subVariants.archetypalProblem: All-pairs reachability on small graphs

subVariants.name: Bidirectional BFS
subVariants.keyMechanism: Expand from both source and target; meets in the middle
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Word ladder (large graph), shortest path with huge branching factor

subVariants.name: 0-1 BFS
subVariants.keyMechanism: Deque instead of queue; handles edges of weight 0 or 1 only
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Minimum flips in grid, shortest path with free moves

subVariants.name: A*
subVariants.keyMechanism: BFS + admissible heuristic to guide expansion toward goal
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Pathfinding with spatial structure, 8-puzzle

subVariants.name: Implicit graph / state-space BFS
subVariants.keyMechanism: Nodes are (partial) problem states, not literal graph nodes
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Sliding puzzle, lock combination, BFS on game states

redFlags: Graph is too large to store explicitly (use implicit traversal or meet-in-the-middle)
Edge weights are real-valued and large (Dijkstra still works but consider precision)
Problem is about structure of the graph itself, not traversal (use Union-Find or matrix methods)

paradigms.name: Backtracking and Constraint Search
paradigms.coreIdea: Build a solution incrementally; abandon ("prune") partial solutions as soon as they violate constraints. Correct but potentially exponential -- pruning is everything.
whenItApplies: Problem asks to enumerate, find, or count all valid configurations
Constraints eliminate large portions of the search space early
No polynomial algorithm is known (NP-hard in general, tractable with pruning for small N)
"All permutations", "all subsets satisfying X", "place N queens", "solve this puzzle"

subVariants.name: Subset enumeration
subVariants.keyMechanism: Include/exclude each element; 2^N leaves
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Subsets, combination sum, power set

subVariants.name: Permutation generation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Fix element at each position; N! leaves
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Permutations, next permutation

subVariants.name: Constraint propagation (CSP-style)
subVariants.keyMechanism: After each assignment, propagate constraints to reduce domains
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Sudoku solver, N-queens with forward checking

subVariants.name: Branch and bound
subVariants.keyMechanism: Track best solution so far; prune branches that cannot beat it
subVariants.archetypalProblem: 0-1 knapsack (exact), TSP (exact)

subVariants.name: Iterative deepening DFS (IDDFS)
subVariants.keyMechanism: DFS with depth limit that increases; O(b^d) time, O(d) space
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Maze with unknown depth, 15-puzzle

subVariants.name: Meet in the middle
subVariants.keyMechanism: Split search space in half; solve each half, then join
subVariants.archetypalProblem: 4-sum, subset sum with large N, bidirectional backtrack

redFlags: N is large and pruning is weak (exponential blowup; use DP or approximation)
Problem only asks for one solution, not all (often DP or greedy is faster)
The constraint structure has a polynomial algorithm you haven't noticed yet

paradigms.name: Randomized Algorithms
paradigms.coreIdea: Use randomness to simplify the algorithm, avoid adversarial worst cases, or approximate hard problems. Correct in expectation or with high probability.
whenItApplies: Deterministic worst case is bad but expected case matters
Need to sample from a large or streaming population
Problem involves hashing, fingerprinting, or approximate membership
The adversary can break deterministic approaches (use randomized to deny worst-case inputs)

subVariants.name: Randomized quickselect
subVariants.keyMechanism: Random pivot; expected O(n) kth-element
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Kth largest element, median of unsorted array

subVariants.name: Reservoir sampling
subVariants.keyMechanism: Single pass over stream; each element has equal probability of being in sample
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Random sample from unknown-length stream

subVariants.name: Random hashing / universal hashing
subVariants.keyMechanism: Random hash function; low collision probability
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Distributed caching, frequency estimation

subVariants.name: Bloom filter
subVariants.keyMechanism: Hash into multiple bit positions; false positives possible, no false negatives
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Membership test with space constraint

subVariants.name: Count-Min Sketch
subVariants.keyMechanism: Array of hash-indexed counters; approximate frequency queries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Heavy hitters in a stream

subVariants.name: Monte Carlo / Las Vegas
subVariants.keyMechanism: MC: fast, possibly wrong answer; LV: always correct, random runtime
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Primality testing (Miller-Rabin), randomized min-cut (Karger's)

subVariants.name: Skip list
subVariants.keyMechanism: Probabilistic layered linked list; O(log n) expected ops
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Ordered set with fast insert/delete, LRU cache variants

redFlags: Problem requires a guaranteed correct answer with a deterministic bound (use exact algorithm)
Randomness isn't available or seeding is adversarial
The problem size is small enough that deterministic O(n log n) is fine

paradigms.name: Mathematical and Number-Theoretic
paradigms.coreIdea: Exploit algebraic structure, number theory, or combinatorial identities to reduce an apparently hard problem to arithmetic -- often from O(n) to O(log n) or O(1).
whenItApplies: Problem involves modular arithmetic, divisibility, primes, or GCD structure
Counting problems with large N where direct enumeration is impossible
Geometric problems reducible to arithmetic (area, intersections, convex hull)
The answer involves factorials, Catalan numbers, Fibonacci, or similar sequences

subVariants.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes
subVariants.keyMechanism: Mark multiples; O(n log log n) prime generation
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Count primes, prime factorization up to N

subVariants.name: GCD / LCM structure
subVariants.keyMechanism: Euclidean algorithm; Bezout's identity for linear combinations
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Fraction simplification, water jug, minimum steps to make GCD

subVariants.name: Modular exponentiation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Fast power with modular reduction; O(log n)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Large Fibonacci, matrix power mod, RSA-style operations

subVariants.name: Combinatorics (nCr mod p)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Pascal's triangle or Lucas' theorem for modular binomial
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Count valid paths in grid, distributing items

subVariants.name: Pigeonhole principle
subVariants.keyMechanism: If n items in k < n bins, some bin has >= 2
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Existence proofs, duplicate detection, calendar problems

subVariants.name: Prefix sum / difference array
subVariants.keyMechanism: Precompute cumulative sums; O(1) range query after O(n) build
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Subarray sum equals K, range update + query

subVariants.name: Convex hull trick
subVariants.keyMechanism: Envelope of linear functions; O(n) or O(n log n) DP optimization
subVariants.archetypalProblem: DP where cost is linear in a variable, CHT for slope optimization

subVariants.name: Geometric primitives
subVariants.keyMechanism: Cross product for orientation, Shoelace for area, sweep for intersections
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Convex hull, polygon area, point in polygon

redFlags: Problem doesn't have algebraic structure to exploit (use DP or graph instead)
Modular arithmetic introduces precision issues the problem doesn't accommodate
The number-theoretic reduction is more complex than the direct approach for the given constraints

paradigms.name: Simulation and State Machines
paradigms.coreIdea: Model the problem as a sequence of state transitions and execute them directly. No clever reduction -- the algorithm is the specification.
whenItApplies: Problem describes a process with explicit rules ("at each step, do X")
The state space is small enough to enumerate or the simulation terminates quickly
Finding cycles or steady states in a deterministic process
Event-driven systems where future events are triggered by current state

subVariants.name: Direct simulation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Execute the described process step by step; verify termination
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Game of Life, robot on a grid, simulate a stack/queue

subVariants.name: Cycle detection (Floyd / Brent)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Tortoise-and-hare or Brent's algorithm; find loop in O(n) time O(1) space
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Linked list cycle, find duplicate in array, hash function cycle

subVariants.name: Event-driven simulation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Priority queue of future events; advance time to next event
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Task scheduler, meeting room simulation, stock price events

subVariants.name: Finite automaton
subVariants.keyMechanism: Explicit state machine with transition table; process input character by character
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Regex matching (simple), string parsing, valid number

subVariants.name: Cellular automaton
subVariants.keyMechanism: Each cell updates based on neighbors; often run for K steps
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Conway's Game of Life, falling sand, spreading wildfire

subVariants.name: Physics / geometric simulation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Apply movement/collision rules repeatedly; check invariants
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Asteroid collision, dominoes falling, bouncing ball

redFlags: Simulation runs for too many steps (need closed-form math or cycle detection)
Problem asks for the optimal sequence of steps, not just the result of a given sequence (use DP or search)
State space is too large to track explicitly (use hashing or abstraction)

paradigms.name: Layered Architecture
paradigms.coreIdea: Separate concerns into layers with dependency rules, where each layer only depends on layers below it, enabling testability and independent evolution of each layer.
whenItApplies: Complex domains needing testability and separation of concerns
Long-lived codebases requiring independent evolution of components
Systems where business logic must be isolated from infrastructure
Applications requiring multiple deployment targets or UI frontends

subVariants.name: Hexagonal / Ports-and-Adapters
subVariants.keyMechanism: Core domain defines ports (interfaces); adapters implement them for specific technologies
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Swapping databases or UI frameworks without changing business logic

subVariants.name: Clean Architecture
subVariants.keyMechanism: Concentric rings with dependency rule pointing inward; entities at center, frameworks at edge
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Enterprise applications with complex domain logic and multiple delivery mechanisms

subVariants.name: Onion Architecture
subVariants.keyMechanism: Domain model at core, domain services wrap it, application services wrap those, infrastructure outermost
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Domain-driven design implementations with rich domain models

subVariants.name: Vertical Slice
subVariants.keyMechanism: Organize by feature rather than layer; each slice contains all layers for one use case
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Feature-oriented teams working independently on distinct capabilities

redFlags: Simple CRUD applications where layers add unnecessary indirection
Prototypes or throwaway projects where speed matters more than structure
Small scripts or utilities with minimal business logic
classicProblems: Enterprise applications with complex business rules

paradigms.name: Test Infrastructure
paradigms.coreIdea: Design the test environment as a first-class architectural concern, ensuring tests are reliable, isolated, and representative of production behavior.
whenItApplies: E2E testing requiring realistic environment simulation
Integration testing across multiple system boundaries
CI pipeline reliability and reproducibility requirements
Applications with complex external dependencies (filesystem, network, databases)

subVariants.name: Sandbox patterns
subVariants.keyMechanism: Temporary directories, mock filesystems, and isolated environments that reset between tests
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Electron app testing, CLI tool testing with filesystem side effects

subVariants.name: Fixture factories
subVariants.keyMechanism: Programmatic creation of test data with sensible defaults and composable overrides
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Database integration tests requiring complex entity graphs

subVariants.name: Environment isolation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Containers, VM snapshots, or process-level isolation ensuring no test-to-test contamination
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Parallel CI pipelines running tests against shared infrastructure

subVariants.name: Test doubles at boundaries
subVariants.keyMechanism: Mocks, stubs, and fakes placed at architectural boundaries to control external behavior
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Testing payment processing without hitting real payment APIs

redFlags: Unit-test-only projects with no external dependencies
Pure functions or stateless services with trivial test setup
Projects where test infrastructure cost exceeds application complexity

paradigms.name: Event-Driven Architecture
paradigms.coreIdea: Components communicate through events, decoupling producers from consumers and enabling asynchronous, reactive workflows.
whenItApplies: Asynchronous workflows where producers should not wait for consumers
Audit trail requirements where every state change must be recorded
Multi-consumer scenarios where the same event triggers different reactions
Systems requiring temporal decoupling between components

subVariants.name: Pub/Sub
subVariants.keyMechanism: Publishers emit events to topics; subscribers receive all events on subscribed topics
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Notification systems, real-time dashboards

subVariants.name: Message Bus
subVariants.keyMechanism: Central bus routes messages between producers and consumers with guaranteed delivery
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Microservice integration, order processing pipelines

subVariants.name: Event Sourcing
subVariants.keyMechanism: Store state as an append-only sequence of events; rebuild current state by replaying
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Financial ledgers, audit-critical systems, temporal queries

subVariants.name: CQRS
subVariants.keyMechanism: Separate command (write) and query (read) models optimized for their respective access patterns
subVariants.archetypalProblem: High-read/low-write systems, complex reporting alongside transactional writes

subVariants.name: Domain Events
subVariants.keyMechanism: Business-meaningful events emitted by domain objects to trigger side effects outside the aggregate
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Order placed triggers inventory reservation, email notification, and analytics

redFlags: Simple request-response interactions with no async needs
Low-latency requirements where event routing adds unacceptable overhead
Systems where eventual consistency is not acceptable
classicProblems: Order processing with multiple downstream services

paradigms.name: Plugin and Extension
paradigms.coreIdea: Design for extensibility through well-defined extension points, allowing behavior to be added or modified without changing core code.
whenItApplies: Configurable behavior that varies by deployment or customer
Third-party extensibility requirements
Core framework with pluggable implementations
Cross-cutting concerns applied conditionally

subVariants.name: Strategy Pattern
subVariants.keyMechanism: Define a family of algorithms behind an interface; swap implementations at runtime
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Payment processors, sorting strategies, rendering engines

subVariants.name: Hook Systems
subVariants.keyMechanism: Named lifecycle hooks that plugins register callbacks for; core invokes at defined points
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Git hooks, React lifecycle, WordPress actions/filters

subVariants.name: Middleware Chains
subVariants.keyMechanism: Ordered pipeline of handlers; each can transform request/response or short-circuit
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Express middleware, Redux middleware, HTTP interceptors

subVariants.name: Plugin Registries
subVariants.keyMechanism: Central registry discovers and loads plugins by convention; plugins declare capabilities via manifest
subVariants.archetypalProblem: VS Code extensions, webpack plugins, Babel plugins

redFlags: Fixed behavior with no variation across deployments or users
Simple applications where extension points add unnecessary complexity
Performance-critical paths where indirection cost matters

paradigms.name: Relational Modeling
paradigms.coreIdea: Structure data using normalized relations with referential integrity, optimizing for consistency, complex queries, and ACID transactions.
whenItApplies: Complex queries involving joins across multiple entities
ACID transaction requirements for data integrity
Reporting and analytics workloads with ad-hoc queries
Well-defined schemas with stable relationships

subVariants.name: Normalization (3NF, BCNF)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Decompose tables to eliminate redundancy; each fact stored once
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Transactional systems with frequent writes and strict consistency

subVariants.name: Star Schema
subVariants.keyMechanism: Central fact table surrounded by dimension tables; optimized for analytical queries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Data warehouses, business intelligence dashboards

subVariants.name: EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Generic triple store within relational database; flexible but query-expensive
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Product catalogs with variable attributes, medical records

subVariants.name: Temporal Tables
subVariants.keyMechanism: Track historical state with valid-time and transaction-time columns
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Audit trails, regulatory compliance, point-in-time queries

redFlags: Unstructured or highly variable data without clear schema
Extreme write throughput requirements where normalization creates bottlenecks
Simple key-value access patterns with no joins

paradigms.name: Document Modeling
paradigms.coreIdea: Store data as self-contained documents optimized for read patterns, trading normalization for query simplicity and schema flexibility.
whenItApplies: Flexible schemas where document structure varies across records
Hierarchical data that maps naturally to nested documents
Read-heavy workloads where denormalization improves performance
Rapid iteration where schema evolution must be frictionless

subVariants.name: Embedded Documents
subVariants.keyMechanism: Nest related data within a single document; single read retrieves everything
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Blog posts with comments, user profiles with addresses

subVariants.name: Referenced Documents
subVariants.keyMechanism: Store IDs linking to other documents; application-level joins
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Many-to-many relationships, shared entities across collections

subVariants.name: Schema-on-Read
subVariants.keyMechanism: No enforced schema at write time; structure interpreted at query time
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Log ingestion, IoT telemetry, data lake queries

subVariants.name: Polymorphic Documents
subVariants.keyMechanism: Single collection stores documents of different shapes; discriminator field identifies type
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Content management with mixed content types, event stores

redFlags: Complex joins across multiple document types are frequent
Strict consistency and referential integrity requirements
Write patterns that update the same field across many documents

paradigms.name: Polyglot Persistence
paradigms.coreIdea: Use different storage engines for different data access patterns, optimizing each workload with the best-fit technology.
whenItApplies: Diverse access patterns within a single application (transactional + search + caching)
Performance optimization requiring specialized storage per workload
Systems with both structured and unstructured data
Scaling requirements that differ across data types

subVariants.name: SQL + NoSQL Hybrid
subVariants.keyMechanism: Relational database for transactional data alongside document store for flexible data
subVariants.archetypalProblem: E-commerce with SQL for orders and MongoDB for product catalogs

subVariants.name: Read/Write Store Separation
subVariants.keyMechanism: Optimized write store (normalized) feeds an optimized read store (denormalized)
subVariants.archetypalProblem: CQRS implementations, reporting databases fed by event streams

subVariants.name: Search Engine Overlay
subVariants.keyMechanism: Primary database synced to a search engine for full-text and faceted queries
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Product search with Elasticsearch alongside PostgreSQL for transactions

subVariants.name: Cache Layer
subVariants.keyMechanism: In-memory cache (Redis, Memcached) fronting a persistent store for hot data
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Session storage, leaderboards, rate limiting alongside persistent user data

redFlags: Small teams where operational burden of multiple databases is unsustainable
Simple access patterns served well by a single database
Data consistency requirements that make synchronization between stores prohibitive
classicProblems: E-commerce platforms (SQL for orders, Redis for sessions, Elasticsearch for search)
Social networks (graph DB for relationships, blob store for media, cache for feeds)
Analytics platforms (time-series DB for metrics, SQL for configuration, S3 for raw data)

paradigms.name: Resource-Oriented
paradigms.coreIdea: Model the API as resources with standard operations, leveraging HTTP semantics for CRUD-dominant interactions with multiple clients.
whenItApplies: CRUD-dominant data models with well-defined entities
Multiple client types (web, mobile, third-party) consuming the same API
Cacheable responses where HTTP caching reduces server load
Public APIs requiring broad developer accessibility

subVariants.name: REST
subVariants.keyMechanism: Resources identified by URIs; standard HTTP methods map to operations; stateless interactions
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Public APIs, mobile backends, content management APIs

subVariants.name: HATEOAS
subVariants.keyMechanism: Responses include hypermedia links guiding clients to available state transitions
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Workflow-driven APIs, discoverable APIs with evolving capabilities

subVariants.name: Resource Nesting
subVariants.keyMechanism: Child resources nested under parent URIs to express ownership and containment
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Comments under posts, line items under orders

subVariants.name: Bulk Operations
subVariants.keyMechanism: Batch endpoints for creating, updating, or deleting multiple resources in one request
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Import/export features, batch status updates, data migration endpoints

redFlags: Complex workflows that don't map to CRUD operations
Real-time bidirectional communication needs
Highly interconnected data requiring multiple round trips to assemble
classicProblems: Internal service APIs with well-defined entity models

paradigms.name: Query-Oriented
paradigms.coreIdea: Let clients specify exactly what data they need, reducing over-fetching and enabling rapid frontend iteration against complex data graphs.
whenItApplies: Complex data graphs where different views need different subsets
Bandwidth-sensitive clients (mobile) needing minimal payloads
Rapid frontend iteration where backend changes should be minimal
Aggregation of data from multiple backend services into a single response

subVariants.name: GraphQL
subVariants.keyMechanism: Typed schema with resolver functions; clients send queries specifying exact fields needed
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Social feeds, dashboards aggregating multiple data sources

subVariants.name: Query Builders
subVariants.keyMechanism: Structured query parameters allowing filtering, sorting, and field selection via URL or body
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Admin panels, report builders, data exploration tools

subVariants.name: Field Selection
subVariants.keyMechanism: Sparse fieldsets allowing clients to request only specific fields from REST-like endpoints
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Mobile APIs optimizing payload size, list views vs detail views

subVariants.name: Persisted Queries
subVariants.keyMechanism: Pre-registered query templates identified by hash; eliminates query parsing overhead and attack surface
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Production GraphQL with security constraints and performance requirements

redFlags: Simple data models where REST endpoints suffice
Caching-critical systems where query variability defeats cache hit rates
Small teams where GraphQL infrastructure overhead is not justified
classicProblems: Social media feeds with variable content types
Dashboard applications aggregating multiple data sources
Developer portals with complex data exploration

paradigms.name: RPC-Oriented
paradigms.coreIdea: Expose operations as typed function calls across process boundaries, prioritizing type safety, performance, and developer ergonomics for internal services.
whenItApplies: Internal microservice communication with high call volume
Type safety is critical across service boundaries
Streaming data (server-push, client-push, or bidirectional)
Performance-sensitive paths where protocol overhead matters

subVariants.name: gRPC (Protobuf)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Protocol Buffers define service contracts; HTTP/2 transport with streaming support
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Microservice mesh communication, mobile-to-server with code generation

subVariants.name: tRPC (TypeScript)
subVariants.keyMechanism: TypeScript types shared between client and server; no code generation needed
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Full-stack TypeScript applications, Next.js API routes

subVariants.name: JSON-RPC
subVariants.keyMechanism: Lightweight JSON-based RPC over HTTP or WebSocket; simple request-response with method names
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Ethereum node APIs, language server protocol, simple internal services

subVariants.name: Apache Thrift
subVariants.keyMechanism: Cross-language service framework with IDL and multiple transport/protocol options
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Polyglot microservice environments (Facebook, Apache ecosystem)

redFlags: Browser clients without a proxy layer to handle binary protocols
Human-readable debugging requirements where binary formats are opaque
Public APIs where REST discoverability is more important than performance

paradigms.name: Distributed Transaction
paradigms.coreIdea: Coordinate state changes across multiple services, ensuring consistency when a single database transaction is not possible.
whenItApplies: Multi-service writes that must succeed or fail together
Business processes spanning multiple bounded contexts
Consistency requirements beyond what eventual consistency provides
Compensating action workflows where partial failure must be undone

subVariants.name: SAGA (Choreography)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Each service publishes events triggering the next step; compensating events handle rollback
subVariants.archetypalProblem: E-commerce order flow: reserve inventory -> charge payment -> ship

subVariants.name: SAGA (Orchestration)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Central orchestrator coordinates steps and compensations; explicit workflow definition
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Travel booking: flight + hotel + car with coordinated cancellation

subVariants.name: Two-Phase Commit (2PC)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Coordinator asks all participants to prepare, then commit or rollback atomically
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Distributed database writes, XA transactions across data sources

subVariants.name: TCC (Try-Confirm-Cancel)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Reserve resources (try), confirm reservation, or cancel if any step fails
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Seat reservation systems, inventory holds with timeout

subVariants.name: Transactional Outbox
subVariants.keyMechanism: Write events to an outbox table in the same transaction as data; relay process publishes them
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Reliable event publishing without dual-write problems

redFlags: Single database where local transactions suffice
Eventual consistency is acceptable and simpler patterns work
Low-volume operations where manual reconciliation is feasible
classicProblems: Order fulfillment across inventory, payment, and shipping services
Financial transfers between accounts in different systems

paradigms.name: Consensus Protocol
paradigms.coreIdea: Achieve agreement among distributed nodes despite failures, ensuring consistent state across replicas or coordinated cluster behavior.
whenItApplies: Distributed databases requiring consistent replication
Leader election in clustered systems
Replicated state machines needing deterministic execution
Conflict resolution in multi-writer scenarios

subVariants.name: Raft
subVariants.keyMechanism: Leader-based consensus with term-based elections and log replication; designed for understandability
subVariants.archetypalProblem: etcd, Consul, distributed key-value stores

subVariants.name: Paxos
subVariants.keyMechanism: Proposers, acceptors, and learners reach agreement through two-phase protocol; theoretical foundation
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Google Chubby, foundational consensus in distributed systems courses

subVariants.name: CRDT (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Data structures that merge concurrent updates without coordination; eventual consistency guaranteed
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Collaborative editing, shopping carts, distributed counters

subVariants.name: Vector Clocks
subVariants.keyMechanism: Logical timestamps tracking causal ordering of events across nodes
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Conflict detection in distributed databases (Dynamo-style), causal message ordering

redFlags: Single-node systems where local concurrency primitives suffice
Strong consistency not needed and simpler eventual consistency works
Low-scale systems where consensus overhead is disproportionate

paradigms.name: Actor Model
paradigms.coreIdea: Encapsulate state and behavior in actors that communicate exclusively via asynchronous messages, providing natural concurrency and fault isolation.
whenItApplies: High concurrency with many independent stateful entities
Fault isolation where one component's failure must not cascade
Natural message-passing domains where entities interact asynchronously
Systems requiring supervision hierarchies for self-healing

subVariants.name: Erlang/OTP-style
subVariants.keyMechanism: Lightweight processes with mailboxes; supervisors restart failed processes; let-it-crash philosophy
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Telecom switches, WhatsApp messaging, real-time bidding

subVariants.name: Akka
subVariants.keyMechanism: JVM actor framework with typed actors, clustering, and persistence; supervision trees
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Financial trading systems, IoT device management

subVariants.name: Virtual Actors (Orleans)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Actors activated on demand and transparently placed across cluster; no explicit lifecycle management
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Game servers (Halo), IoT digital twins, shopping cart per user

subVariants.name: Process Supervision Trees
subVariants.keyMechanism: Hierarchical supervisors define restart strategies (one-for-one, one-for-all) for child actors
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Self-healing services, connection pool management, workflow engines

redFlags: Synchronous request-response workflows where message-passing adds complexity
Shared-state-critical operations where actors must coordinate frequently
Simple stateless services where actor overhead is unnecessary
classicProblems: Telecom systems with millions of concurrent sessions

paradigms.name: Rendering Architecture
paradigms.coreIdea: Choose when and where HTML is generated to balance SEO, time-to-interactive, and developer experience based on content and interaction patterns.
whenItApplies: SEO requirements demanding server-rendered HTML
Time-to-interactive targets for content-heavy sites
Hybrid applications with both static and dynamic content
Progressive enhancement strategies for diverse client capabilities

subVariants.name: SSR (Server-Side Rendering)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Server generates full HTML per request; client hydrates for interactivity
subVariants.archetypalProblem: E-commerce product pages, news sites, landing pages

subVariants.name: CSR (Client-Side Rendering)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Server sends minimal HTML; JavaScript renders everything in the browser
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Dashboards, admin panels, highly interactive applications

subVariants.name: ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Static pages regenerated on-demand after stale time; combines static performance with dynamic freshness
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Blogs, documentation sites, product catalogs with infrequent updates

subVariants.name: Streaming SSR
subVariants.keyMechanism: Server streams HTML chunks as data becomes available; progressive rendering without waiting for all data
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Data-heavy pages with slow backend services, personalized content

subVariants.name: Islands Architecture
subVariants.keyMechanism: Static HTML with isolated interactive components (islands) hydrated independently
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Content-heavy sites with isolated interactive widgets (Astro, Fresh)

redFlags: Fully interactive applications where CSR is sufficient and simpler
Internal tools where SEO is irrelevant
Simple static sites where a basic static generator works
classicProblems: E-commerce storefronts balancing SEO and interactivity
Content publishing platforms with dynamic personalization
Documentation sites with search and navigation

paradigms.name: Component Architecture
paradigms.coreIdea: Structure UI as composable, reusable building blocks with well-defined interfaces, enabling design system consistency and independent development.
whenItApplies: Design systems requiring consistent UI patterns across applications
Shared component libraries consumed by multiple teams
Complex UIs with deeply nested interactive elements
Applications requiring accessible, theme-able UI primitives

subVariants.name: Atomic Design
subVariants.keyMechanism: Hierarchy of atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, and pages; bottom-up composition
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Design system creation, style guide documentation

subVariants.name: Compound Components
subVariants.keyMechanism: Parent component shares implicit state with children; flexible composition with coordinated behavior
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Tabs, accordions, select dropdowns with customizable rendering

subVariants.name: Render Props / Slots
subVariants.keyMechanism: Components accept functions or templates that control rendering of internal parts
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Data tables with custom cell rendering, virtualized lists

subVariants.name: Headless UI
subVariants.keyMechanism: Logic and accessibility separated from presentation; consumers provide their own styling
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Accessible comboboxes, date pickers, and menus across different design systems

redFlags: One-off pages with no reuse potential
Simple forms where component abstraction adds overhead
Prototypes where speed matters more than reusability
classicProblems: Enterprise design systems (Material UI, Ant Design)
Form builders with dynamic field types
Data table components with sorting, filtering, and pagination

paradigms.name: Cache Architecture
paradigms.coreIdea: Store computed results closer to consumers to reduce latency and load, applying caching at the appropriate tier for the access pattern.
whenItApplies: Read-heavy workloads where the same data is requested repeatedly
Latency-sensitive applications with performance SLAs
Expensive computations or slow backend queries that can be reused
Distributed systems needing to reduce cross-service call volume

subVariants.name: CDN Edge Cache
subVariants.keyMechanism: Cache static and dynamic content at edge locations geographically close to users
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Static asset delivery, API response caching for global users

subVariants.name: Application Cache (In-Memory LRU)
subVariants.keyMechanism: In-process cache with eviction policy; fastest access, limited by process memory
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Hot configuration data, parsed templates, computed aggregations

subVariants.name: Database Query Cache
subVariants.keyMechanism: Cache query results at the database or ORM layer; invalidate on writes
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Repeated analytical queries, slow join results, materialized views

subVariants.name: Multi-Tier Cache
subVariants.keyMechanism: L1 (in-process) -> L2 (distributed Redis) -> L3 (database); check each tier in order
subVariants.archetypalProblem: High-traffic web applications needing both speed and capacity

redFlags: Write-heavy workloads where cache invalidation dominates
Consistency-critical data where stale reads are unacceptable
Small datasets that fit entirely in memory without caching infrastructure
classicProblems: Content delivery for global web applications
API response caching with TTL management
Session storage and user preference caching
Database query result caching for dashboards

paradigms.name: Optimization Approach
paradigms.coreIdea: Reduce resource consumption through deferred, batched, or eliminated computation, applied when measurable performance targets are not being met.
whenItApplies: Performance budgets that are being exceeded
Large bundles causing slow initial load times
Cold start issues in serverless or container environments
Resource contention under high concurrency

subVariants.name: Lazy Loading
subVariants.keyMechanism: Defer loading of resources until they are needed; reduce initial payload and startup time
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Image loading below the fold, route-based code splitting in SPAs

subVariants.name: Prefetching
subVariants.keyMechanism: Predict and load resources before they are requested; trade bandwidth for perceived speed
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Link prefetch on hover, predictive data loading, DNS prefetch

subVariants.name: Code Splitting
subVariants.keyMechanism: Break application bundle into chunks loaded on demand; reduce initial JavaScript payload
subVariants.archetypalProblem: SPA route splitting, dynamic imports for heavy features

subVariants.name: Connection Pooling
subVariants.keyMechanism: Reuse pre-established connections instead of creating new ones per request
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Database connection management, HTTP keep-alive, gRPC channel reuse

subVariants.name: Request Coalescing
subVariants.keyMechanism: Combine multiple concurrent requests for the same resource into a single backend call
subVariants.archetypalProblem: DataLoader pattern in GraphQL, singleflight in Go, cache stampede prevention

redFlags: Already meeting performance targets (premature optimization)
Complexity cost exceeds performance gain
Development-only environments where optimization obscures debugging
classicProblems: SPA bundle optimization and code splitting

paradigms.name: Auth Architecture
paradigms.coreIdea: Verify identity and establish trust for system access, choosing the authentication mechanism that matches the client type, security requirements, and user experience goals.
whenItApplies: User-facing applications requiring login and session management
Service-to-service authentication in distributed systems
Multi-tenant systems needing identity isolation
API access requiring credential management

subVariants.name: Session-Based (Server-Side)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Server stores session state; client sends session ID via cookie; server validates on each request
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Traditional web applications, server-rendered sites

subVariants.name: Token-Based (JWT)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Self-contained signed tokens carrying claims; stateless server validation
subVariants.archetypalProblem: SPA authentication, mobile app auth, stateless API access

subVariants.name: OAuth2/OIDC Flows
subVariants.keyMechanism: Delegated authorization and federated identity; authorization code, client credentials, device flows
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Social login, third-party API access, enterprise SSO

subVariants.name: API Keys
subVariants.keyMechanism: Long-lived secret tokens identifying the calling application; simple but limited
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Developer API access, service integration, webhook verification

subVariants.name: mTLS (Mutual TLS)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Both client and server present certificates; transport-layer identity verification
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Service mesh authentication, zero-trust network access

redFlags: Public read-only APIs where authentication adds unnecessary friction
Internal tools in trusted networks where simpler mechanisms suffice
Prototypes where auth complexity delays core feature development
classicProblems: SaaS application login and session management

paradigms.name: Authorization Model
paradigms.coreIdea: Determine what authenticated entities are allowed to do, selecting the model that matches the complexity of permission hierarchies and audit requirements.
whenItApplies: Complex permission hierarchies with multiple roles and scopes
Multi-tenant systems where data isolation is critical
Audit requirements demanding explainable access decisions
Dynamic permissions based on resource relationships or attributes

subVariants.name: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Permissions assigned to roles; users assigned to roles; simple and widely understood
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Admin/editor/viewer access levels, departmental access control

subVariants.name: ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Access decisions based on attributes of user, resource, and environment; highly flexible
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Time-based access, location-based restrictions, classification-based document access

subVariants.name: ReBAC (Relationship-Based Access Control)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Access determined by relationships between entities in a graph; supports inheritance and nesting
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Google Drive sharing model, organizational hierarchies, social network privacy

subVariants.name: Policy-as-Code (OPA/Cedar)
subVariants.keyMechanism: Authorization rules defined in a declarative policy language; evaluated by a policy engine
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Enterprise IAM with auditable policies, multi-service authorization consistency

redFlags: Single-role applications where owner-only access is sufficient
Simple public/private visibility without granular permissions
Small applications where hardcoded checks are clear and maintainable
classicProblems: Healthcare systems with patient data access controls

paradigms.name: Deployment Model
paradigms.coreIdea: Strategy for releasing new versions with controlled risk, balancing deployment speed with the ability to detect and recover from problems.
whenItApplies: Production deployments requiring zero or minimal downtime
Gradual rollout needs to limit blast radius of defects
A/B testing or feature experimentation requirements
Regulated environments requiring deployment audit trails

subVariants.name: Blue-Green
subVariants.keyMechanism: Two identical environments; deploy to inactive, swap traffic atomically, keep old as rollback
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Database migration deployments, critical service updates

subVariants.name: Canary
subVariants.keyMechanism: Route small percentage of traffic to new version; monitor metrics; gradually increase
subVariants.archetypalProblem: SaaS releases with error rate monitoring, API version rollouts

subVariants.name: Rolling
subVariants.keyMechanism: Replace instances incrementally; always some old and some new running during deployment
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Kubernetes deployments, auto-scaling group updates

subVariants.name: Feature Flags
subVariants.keyMechanism: Deploy code dark; enable features per user/segment via runtime configuration
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Trunk-based development, gradual feature rollout, kill switches

subVariants.name: A/B Testing
subVariants.keyMechanism: Split traffic between variants; measure business metrics to determine winner
subVariants.archetypalProblem: UI experiments, pricing page optimization, onboarding flow testing

redFlags: Development-only environments where deployment strategy is irrelevant
Single-user systems with no uptime requirements
Batch jobs where scheduled restarts are acceptable

paradigms.name: Scaling Architecture
paradigms.coreIdea: Handle increasing load by adding capacity, choosing the scaling strategy that matches the workload characteristics and cost constraints.
whenItApplies: Traffic growth exceeding single-instance capacity
Variable load patterns with peaks and valleys
Cost optimization requiring right-sizing of resources
Availability requirements demanding redundancy

subVariants.name: Horizontal Scaling
subVariants.keyMechanism: Add more instances behind a load balancer; requires stateless or externalized state
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Web application servers, API gateways, worker pools

subVariants.name: Vertical Scaling
subVariants.keyMechanism: Increase CPU, memory, or storage of existing instances; simpler but has upper limits
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Database servers, in-memory caches, single-threaded bottlenecks

subVariants.name: Serverless/FaaS
subVariants.keyMechanism: Cloud provider manages scaling to zero and up; pay per invocation; no server management
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Event-driven processing, webhook handlers, scheduled tasks

subVariants.name: Edge Compute
subVariants.keyMechanism: Run computation at CDN edge locations close to users; reduce latency for geographically distributed users
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Personalization at edge, A/B testing, auth token validation

redFlags: Fixed load with no growth trajectory where scaling adds unnecessary complexity
Single-machine workloads that are not resource-constrained
Development and testing environments
classicProblems: API services with variable request rates

paradigms.name: Observability Pillar
paradigms.coreIdea: Gain insight into system behavior through telemetry, choosing the observability signal that best answers the questions you need to ask about your system.
whenItApplies: Production systems requiring operational visibility
Debugging distributed system issues across service boundaries
SLO management and reliability engineering
Performance optimization requiring data-driven decisions

subVariants.name: Metrics-Driven
subVariants.keyMechanism: Numeric time-series data (counters, gauges, histograms) aggregated and alerted on
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Dashboard monitoring, SLO tracking, capacity planning

subVariants.name: Log-Driven
subVariants.keyMechanism: Structured event records with context; searchable and filterable for investigation
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Error investigation, audit trails, debugging request flows

subVariants.name: Trace-Driven
subVariants.keyMechanism: Distributed traces following requests across service boundaries with timing data
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Latency investigation in microservices, dependency mapping, bottleneck identification

subVariants.name: Profile-Driven
subVariants.keyMechanism: CPU, memory, and allocation profiling to identify hot paths and resource consumption
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Memory leak detection, CPU hotspot optimization, garbage collection tuning

subVariants.name: Continuous Profiling
subVariants.keyMechanism: Always-on low-overhead profiling in production; correlate profiles with traces and metrics
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Production performance regression detection, cost attribution per endpoint

redFlags: Development-only environments where print debugging suffices
Simple single-process applications with obvious failure modes
Cost-constrained environments where telemetry overhead is prohibitive
classicProblems: Incident response and root cause analysis
SLO definition and error budget tracking

paradigms.name: Monitoring Philosophy
paradigms.coreIdea: Structured approach to defining what to monitor and how to respond, reducing alert fatigue and ensuring actionable observability.
whenItApplies: Establishing monitoring strategy for new services
Alert fatigue reduction when too many non-actionable alerts fire
SRE practices requiring systematic reliability measurement
On-call optimization to reduce toil and improve response time

subVariants.name: USE Method
subVariants.keyMechanism: For every resource: check Utilization, Saturation, and Errors; systematic bottleneck identification
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Infrastructure monitoring, capacity planning, hardware troubleshooting

subVariants.name: RED Method
subVariants.keyMechanism: For every service: track Rate, Errors, and Duration; user-facing health at a glance
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Microservice health dashboards, API monitoring, request-driven services

subVariants.name: Golden Signals
subVariants.keyMechanism: Latency, traffic, errors, and saturation as the four essential signals for any service
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Google SRE monitoring, service-level dashboards, on-call runbooks

subVariants.name: SLO-Based Alerting
subVariants.keyMechanism: Alert on error budget burn rate rather than individual failures; reduces noise, focuses on user impact
subVariants.archetypalProblem: Multi-window burn rate alerts, error budget policies, reliability contracts

redFlags: Ad-hoc monitoring is sufficient for the system's scale and criticality
Single-developer projects where formal monitoring methodology adds overhead
Non-production environments where monitoring investment is premature
classicProblems: On-call alert optimization and fatigue reduction

crossParadigmGuide.signal: "Does X exist?" with large search space
Answer is a single number derivable analytically
Async workflows or event audit trail
Multiple storage engines for different workloads
Complex data graph with bandwidth-sensitive clients
